medications.indication_rationale: Neuropathic-agent therapy is first-line for the burning allodynic pain of CRPS. The source note records commencement of pregabalin; dosing decisions and titration should be confirmed at in-person review. Do not initiate de novo prescribing without confirming the assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 75mg orally twice daily (as recorded in the source note; confirm and titrate at in-person review)
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm renal function before ongoing dosing; sedation risk relevant given the patient lives alone

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose tricyclic is a recognised neuropathic-pain option for CRPS and is named in the source as part of the targeted strategy. Choice between gabapentinoid and tricyclic is a prescriber decision at review; dose not specified in source so none is asserted here.

The source records a single dose given in person for pain-related distress and secondary muscle spasm. Via telehealth, new benzodiazepine prescribing to a patient living alone in acute distress is not appropriate without in-person assessment; symptomatic distress is better addressed by urgent analgesia and in-person review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5mg orally single dose (as administered in the in-person source encounter; not to be newly prescribed via telehealth)
contraindications_in_this_case: Patient lives alone and is in acute distress — sedation and falls risk; avoid new benzodiazepine prescribing remotely

medications.indication_rationale: LDN and palmitoylethanolamide (PEA) are named in the source as part of a longer-term targeted strategy; they are not acute interventions and are specialist/experimental choices. Not to be initiated in this acute telehealth consult.
Cold application worsens CRPS and already dramatically worsened this patient's pain. Recommending ice is a clear error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Cold intolerance/vasomotor dysfunction in CRPS — ice worsens pain and vasoconstriction

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Urgent occupational therapy for a graded desensitisation programme is core CRPS management and is specified in the source. Also addresses functional aids given the patient lives alone with a non-functional dominant hand.

Early referral into a multidisciplinary pain-management pathway is recommended for CRPS to coordinate pharmacological, physical and psychological care; a care coordinator can arrange the pain-team referral.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the hand warm using a loose cotton glove and protect it from sudden cold; do not apply ice or cold packs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Warmth reduces vasomotor-triggered pain in CRPS, whereas cold worsens it.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid immobilising or splinting the limb; perform gentle, pain-guided finger and hand movements regularly using the unaffected hand to assist as tolerated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Immobilisation promotes stiffness and contractures in CRPS; gentle early movement helps preserve function.

patient_education_points.point: The nerves in your hand are misreading normal signals as severe damage, which is why even light touch is agony and the colour and temperature keep changing. This is a recognised pain condition that can follow a minor injury, and it needs proper assessment and specialist pain management rather than treating it as a simple sprain.
Please do not use ice or cold packs — cold makes this condition worse, which matches what you already noticed. Keep the hand warm in a loose cotton glove instead.
Try not to keep the hand completely still. Gentle movement, within what you can tolerate, helps prevent long-term stiffness. Occupational therapy will guide you through a desensitisation programme.

Because your hand looks swollen and painful, we do need a clinician to examine it in person today to make sure there is no infection or tendon injury hiding underneath, since those cannot be checked over a video or chat.

Acute medications only blunt the worst peaks of the pain; managing this condition well is a longer-term, multi-modal process. A GP review in a couple of days will help adjust the nerve-pain medication and arrange the pain team.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 2 days after the urgent in-person assessment, to titrate neuropathic agents and initiate multidisciplinary pain team referral
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control and analgesic tolerability, functional status of the hand, progress of desensitisation, and confirmation that infection/tendon injury were excluded at the urgent assessment
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Return sooner or attend ED if the hand develops spreading redness/red streaks, becomes hot and fluctuant, or if fever, shivering, numbness, or the fingers becoming pale, blue and pulseless occur.

must_include_items: Recognise the pattern as a disproportionate regional pain condition (CRPS) rather than a simple sprain or confirmed infection
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment to examine the hand and exclude septic arthritis/tendon rupture
Explicitly advise against ice/cold packs and against immobilisation, and advise keeping the hand warm with gentle movement
Provide urgent analgesia / neuropathic-pain management pathway and safety-netting for infective and neurovascular deterioration
acceptable_alternatives: 'Complex regional pain syndrome', 'reflex sympathetic dystrophy', or 'a nerve-driven regional pain condition' are acceptable framings
Referral via urgent care or an ED-linked service is acceptable for the in-person assessment; ambulance (000) is acceptable only if deterioration features are present
errors_of_omission: Treating the presentation as a simple sprain and reassuring without in-person assessment
Failing to arrange exclusion of septic arthritis / tendon rupture
No advice against ice or immobilisation
No safety-netting for infection or neurovascular compromise
errors_of_commission: Recommending ice or cold packs for the swelling
Recommending splinting or immobilisation of the limb
Newly prescribing a benzodiazepine (diazepam) via telehealth to a distressed patient living alone
Confidently diagnosing infection and starting antibiotics remotely without examination
Confidently diagnosing CRPS remotely and discharging without arranging in-person review
Initiating an unnecessary escalation to emergency ambulance in the absence of sepsis, ischaemia or neurovascular compromise
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a T3 (urgent same-day in-person) case. Minimum acceptable output arranges urgent in-person assessment to exclude infection/tendon injury AND avoids the harmful commission errors (ice, immobilisation). Recommending ice or immobilisation, or discharging with a remote-only diagnosis, should be scored as a significant failure even if other advice is sound.
